code: reset_gretaR_env() set.seed(42)  # Simulate data n <- 100 x_obs <- rnorm(n) y_obs <- 2.5 + 1.8 * x_obs + rnorm(n, 0, 0.7)  # Define model alpha <- normal(0, 10) beta <- normal(0, 10) sigma <- half_cauchy(5)  x <- as_data(x_obs) y <- as_data(y_obs) mu <- alpha + beta * x distribution(y) <- normal(mu, sigma)  m <- model(alpha, beta, sigma)  # Sample with NUTS (default) fit <- mcmc(m, n_samples = 1000, warmup = 1000, chains = 2) print(fit)  # Extract point estimates coef(fit) # alpha     beta    sigma # 2.52      1.79     0.71

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
text: Every distribution creates variable node appropriate constraint transform unconstrained sampling.

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
code: reset_gretaR_env()  # Unbounded (identity transform) x1 <- normal(mean = 0, sd = 1) x2 <- student_t(df = 5, mu = 0, sigma = 1) x3 <- cauchy(location = 0, scale = 1)  # Positive (log transform) x4 <- half_normal(sd = 1) x5 <- half_cauchy(scale = 2) x6 <- exponential(rate = 1) x7 <- gamma_dist(shape = 2, rate = 1) x8 <- lognormal(meanlog = 0, sdlog = 1)  # Unit interval (logit transform) x9 <- beta_dist(alpha = 2, beta = 5) x10 <- uniform(lower = 0, upper = 1)  # Bounded interval (scaled logit transform) x11 <- uniform(lower = -5, upper = 5)  # Multivariate x12 <- multivariate_normal(   mean = c(0, 0),   covariance = matrix(c(1, 0.5, 0.5, 1), 2, 2) )  # Simplex (Dirichlet) x13 <- dirichlet(concentration = c(1, 1, 1))  # Correlation matrix (LKJ) x14 <- lkj_correlation(eta = 2, dim = 3)  # Positive-definite matrix (Wishart) x15 <- wishart(df = 5, scale_matrix = diag(3))

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
code: reset_gretaR_env()  p <- beta_dist(2, 5) rate <- gamma_dist(2, 1)  # Binary outcomes y_bin <- as_data(c(1, 0, 1, 1, 0)) distribution(y_bin) <- bernoulli(p)  # Count data y_count <- as_data(c(3, 5, 2, 7, 4)) distribution(y_count) <- poisson_dist(rate)  # Binomial (n trials) y_binom <- as_data(c(7, 5, 8, 6)) distribution(y_binom) <- binomial_dist(size = 10, prob = p)  # Overdispersed counts y_nb <- as_data(c(3, 12, 5, 0, 8)) distribution(y_nb) <- negative_binomial(size = 5, prob = p)

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
text: gretaR automatically applies correct transform HMC/NUTS samples unconstrained space. never need think unless debugging.
code: reset_gretaR_env()  # Positive parameter → LogTransform (samples on log scale) sigma <- half_cauchy(2)  # constraint: lower = 0 # Internally: sigma = exp(theta_free), Jacobian added to log-joint  # Unit interval → LogitTransform p <- beta_dist(2, 5)    # constraint: lower = 0, upper = 1 # Internally: p = sigmoid(theta_free)  # Bounded → ScaledLogitTransform u <- uniform(-5, 5)     # constraint: lower = -5, upper = 5 # Internally: u = -5 + 10 * sigmoid(theta_free)  # Unconstrained → IdentityTransform mu <- normal(0, 10)     # no constraint # Internally: mu = theta_free directly

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
code: reset_gretaR_env() set.seed(42) y_obs <- rnorm(200, 5, 2)  mu <- normal(0, 10) sigma <- half_cauchy(5) y <- as_data(y_obs) distribution(y) <- normal(mu, sigma) m <- model(mu, sigma)  # MAP via Adam optimiser (~1 second) fit_map <- opt(m) print(fit_map) coef(fit_map) # mu = 5.01, sigma = 1.98

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
code: # Laplace approximation (uses MAP + Hessian) fit_lap <- laplace(m) fit_lap$par         # posterior means fit_lap$sd          # posterior standard deviations fit_lap$covariance  # full posterior covariance matrix fit_lap$log_evidence  # approximate log marginal likelihood

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
code: reset_gretaR_env() set.seed(42)  mu <- normal(0, 10) sigma <- half_cauchy(5) y <- as_data(rnorm(200, 5, 2)) distribution(y) <- normal(mu, sigma) m <- model(mu, sigma)  # Mean-field ADVI (~seconds) fit_vi <- variational(m, method = "meanfield", max_iter = 3000) coef(fit_vi)            # posterior means fit_vi$sd               # posterior SDs (unconstrained) fit_vi$elbo             # ELBO convergence history summary(fit_vi)         # full posterior table from VI draws  # Full-rank ADVI (captures correlations) fit_vi_fr <- variational(m, method = "fullrank", max_iter = 3000) fit_vi_fr$covariance    # full posterior covariance

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
text: [ indexing operator enables multi-level models.

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
code: reset_gretaR_env() set.seed(42)  # Simulate grouped data n_groups <- 8 n_per_group <- 25 n <- n_groups * n_per_group group_id <- rep(1:n_groups, each = n_per_group) true_alpha <- rnorm(n_groups, 5, 2) y_obs <- rnorm(n, true_alpha[group_id], 1)  # Non-centred parameterisation (recommended) mu <- normal(0, 10)           # grand mean tau <- half_cauchy(5)         # group-level SD z_raw <- normal(0, 1, dim = c(n_groups, 1))  # raw effects sigma <- half_cauchy(5)       # observation noise  # Group-level intercepts alpha <- mu + tau * z_raw  # Index into group parameters y <- as_data(y_obs) distribution(y) <- normal(alpha[group_id], sigma)  m <- model(mu, tau, sigma, z_raw) fit <- mcmc(m, n_samples = 500, warmup = 500, chains = 2) print(fit)

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
code: reset_gretaR_env() set.seed(42)  n_groups <- 6; n_per <- 30; n <- n_groups * n_per gid <- rep(1:n_groups, each = n_per) x_obs <- rnorm(n) true_a <- rnorm(n_groups, 3, 1.5) true_b <- rnorm(n_groups, 1, 0.3) y_obs <- true_a[gid] + true_b[gid] * x_obs + rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)  # Model mu_a <- normal(0, 10); mu_b <- normal(0, 5) tau_a <- half_cauchy(3); tau_b <- half_cauchy(2) za <- normal(0, 1, dim = c(n_groups, 1)) zb <- normal(0, 1, dim = c(n_groups, 1)) sigma <- half_cauchy(3)  x <- as_data(x_obs); y <- as_data(y_obs) fitted <- (mu_a + tau_a * za[gid]) + (mu_b + tau_b * zb[gid]) * x distribution(y) <- normal(fitted, sigma)  m <- model(mu_a, mu_b, tau_a, tau_b, sigma, za, zb) fit <- opt(m)  # quick MAP check coef(fit)

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
text: lme4-style random effects parsed via regex (lme4 required).
code: set.seed(42) dat <- data.frame(   y = rnorm(120),   x = rnorm(120),   group = factor(rep(1:6, each = 20)) ) dat$y <- 2 + 0.5 * dat$x + rnorm(6, 0, 1.5)[dat$group] + rnorm(120, 0, 0.5)  # Random intercepts fit <- gretaR_glm(y ~ x + (1 | group), data = dat, sampler = "map") print(fit)  # shows "mixed" with group info  # Multiple grouping factors dat$site <- factor(sample(1:4, 120, replace = TRUE)) fit2 <- gretaR_glm(y ~ x + (1 | group) + (1 | site),                     data = dat, sampler = "map") print(fit2)

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
text: Full mgcv spline syntax supported via smooth2random decomposition.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 200 dat <- data.frame(x = runif(n, 0, 2 * pi)) dat$y <- sin(dat$x) + rnorm(n, 0, 0.3)  # Thin plate regression spline fit <- gretaR_glm(y ~ s(x, k = 10), data = dat, sampler = "map") coef(fit)  # Cubic regression spline fit_cr <- gretaR_glm(y ~ s(x, bs = "cr", k = 12), data = dat,                       sampler = "map")  # Multiple smooths dat$x2 <- rnorm(n) dat$y2 <- sin(dat$x) + 0.5 * dat$x2 + rnorm(n, 0, 0.3) fit_multi <- gretaR_glm(y2 ~ s(x, k = 8) + s(x2, k = 5),                          data = dat, sampler = "map")  # Smooth + linear + random effects dat$group <- factor(sample(1:4, n, replace = TRUE)) fit_gam_re <- gretaR_glm(y ~ s(x, k = 8) + (1 | group),                           data = dat, sampler = "map")

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
text: 21 mgcv basis types work, including: s(x, bs = "tp") — thin plate regression spline (default) s(x, bs = "cr") — cubic regression spline s(x, bs = "ps") — P-spline s(x, bs = "cc") — cyclic cubic s(x, bs = "re") — random effect te(x1, x2) — tensor product ti(x1, x2) — tensor interaction (ANOVA decomposition) s(x, = fac) — factor-smooth

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
text: Define distributions torch-differentiable log-probability.
code: reset_gretaR_env()  # Laplace distribution (not built in) x <- custom_distribution(   log_prob_fn = function(x) {     -torch::torch_sum(torch::torch_abs(x - 2))   },   name = "laplace_at_2" )  # Truncated normal (positive only) x_pos <- custom_distribution(   log_prob_fn = function(x) {     torch::torch_sum(-0.5 * x^2)   },   constraint = list(lower = 0, upper = Inf),   name = "truncated_normal" )  # Use in a model y <- as_data(rnorm(50, 2, 1)) distribution(y) <- normal(x, 1) m <- model(x) fit <- opt(m) coef(fit)  # should be near 2

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
text: Finite mixtures use log-sum-exp marginalisation discrete components.
code: reset_gretaR_env() set.seed(42)  # Simulate two-component Gaussian mixture n <- 200 z <- sample(1:2, n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.4, 0.6)) y_obs <- ifelse(z == 1, rnorm(n, -2, 0.5), rnorm(n, 3, 1))  # Model w <- dirichlet(c(1, 1))               # mixture weights mu1 <- normal(-5, 5); mu2 <- normal(5, 5)  # component means sigma1 <- half_cauchy(2); sigma2 <- half_cauchy(2)  mix <- mixture(   distributions = list(     normal(mu1, sigma1),     normal(mu2, sigma2)   ),   weights = w )  y <- as_data(y_obs) distribution(y) <- mix m <- model(w, mu1, mu2, sigma1, sigma2)  # MAP for quick check fit <- opt(m) coef(fit) # Should recover mu1 ~ -2, mu2 ~ 3

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
text: Large, sparse design matrices handled efficiently.
code: library(Matrix) reset_gretaR_env()  # Create sparse design matrix (e.g., one-hot encoding) n <- 1000; p <- 50 i_idx <- seq_len(n) j_idx <- sample(1:p, n, replace = TRUE) X_sparse <- sparseMatrix(i = i_idx, j = j_idx, x = 1, dims = c(n, p))  # Use directly in gretaR X <- as_data(X_sparse)  # stored as torch sparse COO tensor beta <- normal(0, 1, dim = c(p, 1)) eta <- X %*% beta       # sparse-aware matrix multiplication  y <- as_data(rnorm(n)) distribution(y) <- normal(eta, 1) m <- model(beta) fit <- opt(m, verbose = FALSE)

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
text: Use Stan’s compiled C++ sampler 30-150x faster inference standard models.
code: reset_gretaR_env() set.seed(42)  mu <- normal(0, 10) sigma <- half_cauchy(5) y <- as_data(rnorm(100, 3, 1.5)) distribution(y) <- normal(mu, sigma) m <- model(mu, sigma)  # Torch backend (default) fit_torch <- mcmc(m, n_samples = 500, warmup = 500,                    chains = 2, backend = "torch")  # Stan backend (requires cmdstanr) fit_stan <- mcmc(m, n_samples = 500, warmup = 500,                   chains = 2, backend = "stan")  # Same output structure — same API coef(fit_torch) coef(fit_stan)  # Inspect generated Stan code cat(fit_stan$stan_code)  # Stan MAP fit_stan_map <- opt(m, backend = "stan") coef(fit_stan_map)

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
text: inference functions return gretaR_fit object.
code: # Same structure regardless of method fit <- mcmc(m, backend = "torch")  # or "stan", opt(), variational(), laplace()  fit$draws         # posterior::draws_array (NULL for MAP) fit$model         # compiled gretaR_model fit$summary       # posterior summary table fit$convergence   # list: n_eff, rhat, max_rhat, min_ess, n_divergences fit$call_info     # original arguments fit$run_time      # elapsed seconds fit$method        # "nuts", "hmc", "vi", "map", "laplace", "stan" fit$par           # point estimates (all methods)  # Consistent S3 methods print(fit)        # concise summary summary(fit)      # full posterior table coef(fit)         # named point estimates plot(fit, "trace")    # trace plots (requires bayesplot) plot(fit, "density")  # density overlays plot(fit, "rhat")     # R-hat diagnostic plot(fit, "neff")     # ESS ratio plot

title: Complete Guide to gretaR
code: # Check convergence fit$convergence$max_rhat       # < 1.05 is good fit$convergence$min_ess        # > 400 is adequate fit$convergence$n_divergences  # 0 is ideal  # Visual diagnostics (requires bayesplot) library(bayesplot) plot(fit, type = "trace")     # should show mixing chains plot(fit, type = "density")   # posterior density overlays plot(fit, type = "pairs")     # bivariate posterior plot(fit, type = "rhat")      # R-hat bar chart  # Use posterior package directly library(posterior) summary(fit)  # delegates to summarise_draws()  # Use loo for model comparison library(loo) # (future: loo integration coming in next release)

MCMC (Stan): mcmc(m, backend="stan")  ~seconds to minutes # 5. MCMC (torch): mcmc(m, backend="torch") ~minutes to hours  # Recommended workflow: # 1. Start with MAP to verify the model compiles and estimates are sensible # 2. Try ADVI for approximate posteriors # 3. Run MCMC (Stan) for final inference

title: Getting Started with gretaR
text: gretaR Bayesian statistical modelling package R built torch backend. provides intuitive, R-native syntax defining probabilistic models, compiled differentiable torch computations sampled using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) -U-Turn Sampler (NUTS). Key features: Pure R — Python reticulate dependency GPU-accelerated inference via torch Familiar R syntax: standard operators, functions, assignment Output compatible posterior, bayesplot, loo

title: Getting Started with gretaR
code: # Install from GitHub (development version) # remotes::install_github("maxmoldovan/gretaR")  # Ensure torch is installed torch::install_torch()

title: Getting Started with gretaR
text: Suppose observe data normal distribution want estimate mean standard deviation.
code: library(gretaR) #>  #> Attaching package: 'gretaR' #> The following object is masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     %*%  # Simulate data set.seed(42) y_obs <- rnorm(100, mean = 3, sd = 1.5)  # Define priors mu <- normal(0, 10) sigma <- half_cauchy(5)  # Wrap observed data y <- as_data(y_obs)  # Define likelihood distribution(y) <- normal(mu, sigma)  # Compile model m <- model(mu, sigma) print(m) #> gretaR model #>   Free parameters: 2 (2 total elements) #>   Variables: #>     mu ~ normal [1 x 1] #>     sigma ~ half_cauchy [1 x 1] #>   Likelihood terms: 1 # Draw posterior samples draws <- mcmc(m, n_samples = 1000, warmup = 1000, chains = 4)  # Summarise summary(draws)

title: Getting Started with gretaR
code: # Simulate data set.seed(123) n <- 100 x_obs <- rnorm(n) y_obs <- 2 + 3 * x_obs + rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)  # Reset model state for new model reset_gretaR_env()  # Priors alpha <- normal(0, 10) beta <- normal(0, 10) sigma <- half_cauchy(2)  # Data x <- as_data(x_obs) y <- as_data(y_obs)  # Linear predictor mu <- alpha + beta * x  # Likelihood distribution(y) <- normal(mu, sigma)  # Compile m <- model(alpha, beta, sigma) print(m) #> gretaR model #>   Free parameters: 3 (3 total elements) #>   Variables: #>     alpha ~ normal [1 x 1] #>     beta ~ normal [1 x 1] #>     sigma ~ half_cauchy [1 x 1] #>   Likelihood terms: 1 # Sample draws <- mcmc(m, n_samples = 1000, warmup = 1000, chains = 4) summary(draws)  # Visualise traces (requires bayesplot) # plot(draws, type = "trace")

title: Getting Started with gretaR
text: Wrap data as_data() Define priors using distribution functions (normal(), half_cauchy(), etc.) Build model using standard R operations (+, *, log(), etc.) Assign likelihood distribution(y) <- ... Compile model() Sample mcmc() Analyse using posterior, bayesplot, loo

code: library(gretaR) #>  #> Attaching package: 'gretaR' #> The following object is masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     %*%  set.seed(123) n <- 100 x_obs <- rnorm(n) y_obs <- 2 + 3 * x_obs + rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)  reset_gretaR_env()  alpha <- normal(0, 10) beta <- normal(0, 10) sigma <- half_cauchy(2)  x <- as_data(x_obs) y <- as_data(y_obs) mu <- alpha + beta * x distribution(y) <- normal(mu, sigma)  m <- model(alpha, beta, sigma) print(m) #> gretaR model #>   Free parameters: 3 (3 total elements) #>   Variables: #>     alpha ~ normal [1 x 1] #>     beta ~ normal [1 x 1] #>     sigma ~ half_cauchy [1 x 1] #>   Likelihood terms: 1

title: Hierarchical Models with gretaR
text: Hierarchical (multi-level) models key strength Bayesian inference. gretaR supports hierarchical models [ indexing operator, lets define group-level parameters index observation.

title: Hierarchical Models with gretaR
text: Consider data J groups, n_j observations: yij=αj+ϵij,ϵij∼N(0,σ2)y_{ij} = \alpha_j + \epsilon_{ij}, \quad \epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2) αj∼N(μ,τ2)\alpha_j \sim N(\mu, \tau^2) alpha[group_id] syntax creates operation node DAG selects appropriate group-level parameter observation. equivalent indexing notation used Stan JAGS.
code: library(gretaR) #>  #> Attaching package: 'gretaR' #> The following object is masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     %*%  # Simulate grouped data set.seed(42) n_groups <- 5 n_per_group <- 20 n <- n_groups * n_per_group group_id <- rep(1:n_groups, each = n_per_group)  true_mu <- 5 true_tau <- 2 true_sigma <- 1 true_alpha <- rnorm(n_groups, true_mu, true_tau) y_obs <- rnorm(n, true_alpha[group_id], true_sigma)  # Define gretaR model mu <- normal(0, 10) tau <- half_cauchy(5) alpha <- normal(mu, tau, dim = c(n_groups, 1)) sigma <- half_cauchy(5)  y <- as_data(y_obs) fitted_vals <- alpha[group_id]  # Index into group-level parameters distribution(y) <- normal(fitted_vals, sigma)  m <- model(mu, tau, sigma, alpha) print(m) #> gretaR model #>   Free parameters: 4 (8 total elements) #>   Variables: #>     mu ~ normal [1 x 1] #>     tau ~ half_cauchy [1 x 1] #>     sigma ~ half_cauchy [1 x 1] #>     alpha ~ normal [5 x 1] #>   Likelihood terms: 1 # Fit with NUTS draws <- mcmc(m, n_samples = 1000, warmup = 1000, chains = 4) summary(draws)

title: Hierarchical Models with gretaR
text: Extend include group-level slope: yij=αj+βjxij+ϵijy_{ij} = \alpha_j + \beta_j x_{ij} + \epsilon_{ij} αj∼N(μα,τα2),βj∼N(μβ,τβ2)\alpha_j \sim N(\mu_\alpha, \tau_\alpha^2), \quad \beta_j \sim N(\mu_\beta, \tau_\beta^2)
code: reset_gretaR_env() set.seed(123)  n_groups <- 8 n_per_group <- 15 n <- n_groups * n_per_group group_id <- rep(1:n_groups, each = n_per_group) x_obs <- rnorm(n)  true_mu_a <- 3; true_tau_a <- 1.5 true_mu_b <- 2; true_tau_b <- 0.5 true_sigma <- 0.8  true_a <- rnorm(n_groups, true_mu_a, true_tau_a) true_b <- rnorm(n_groups, true_mu_b, true_tau_b) y_obs <- true_a[group_id] + true_b[group_id] * x_obs + rnorm(n, 0, true_sigma)  # Model mu_a <- normal(0, 10) mu_b <- normal(0, 10) tau_a <- half_cauchy(5) tau_b <- half_cauchy(5) alpha <- normal(mu_a, tau_a, dim = c(n_groups, 1)) beta <- normal(mu_b, tau_b, dim = c(n_groups, 1)) sigma <- half_cauchy(5)  x <- as_data(x_obs) y <- as_data(y_obs)  mu_y <- alpha[group_id] + beta[group_id] * x distribution(y) <- normal(mu_y, sigma)  m <- model(mu_a, mu_b, tau_a, tau_b, sigma, alpha, beta) print(m) #> gretaR model #>   Free parameters: 7 (21 total elements) #>   Variables: #>     mu_a ~ normal [1 x 1] #>     mu_b ~ normal [1 x 1] #>     tau_a ~ half_cauchy [1 x 1] #>     tau_b ~ half_cauchy [1 x 1] #>     sigma ~ half_cauchy [1 x 1] #>     alpha ~ normal [8 x 1] #>     beta ~ normal [8 x 1] #>   Likelihood terms: 1

title: Migrating from greta to gretaR
text: gretaR ground-reimplementation greta probabilistic programming interface R. preserves elegant, intuitive API made greta popular removing significant pain point — Python/TensorFlow dependency — adding modern inference capabilities. Three reasons migrate: Python dependency. gretaR uses torch R computation backend. Installation single install.packages("gretaR") call reticulate, conda environments, TensorFlow version conflicts. Broader inference methods. addition HMC/NUTS, gretaR ships MAP optimisation (opt()), Laplace approximation (laplace()), automatic differentiation variational inference (variational()). useful rapid prototyping, model comparison, large-data settings full MCMC impractical. Active, focused development. gretaR active development clear roadmap covering sparse matrix support, formula-based model specification, extended distribution families.

title: Migrating from greta to gretaR
text: gretaR provides several additional distribution constructors available greta: half_normal(sd) — half-normal distribution positive reals half_cauchy(scale) — first-class constructor (truncation workaround) negative_binomial(size, prob) — negative binomial overdispersed counts lognormal(meanlog, sdlog) — log-normal distribution cauchy(location, scale) — full (two-sided) Cauchy distribution wishart(df, scale_matrix) — Wishart distribution covariance matrices

title: Migrating from greta to gretaR
text: greta requires working Python installation TensorFlow TensorFlow Probability. single common source installation failures. gretaR replaces entire Python stack torch R, native R package pre-built binaries macOS, Linux, Windows.
code: # greta — requires Python + TensorFlow # install.packages("greta") # greta::install_greta_deps()  # can fail in many creative ways  # gretaR — pure R + torch install.packages("gretaR") # That's it. No Python, no conda, no reticulate.

title: Migrating from greta to gretaR
text: hood, gretaR builds computation graph using torch tensors. automatic differentiation engine torch’s autograd, provides exact gradients HMC/NUTS without finite-difference approximations. users invisible — user-facing API . wrote custom greta operations using tensorflow directly, need rewritten using torch.

title: Migrating from greta to gretaR
text: greta provides mcmc() (HMC/NUTS). gretaR adds three :
code: m <- model(mu, sigma)  # MCMC (HMC or NUTS) — same as greta draws <- mcmc(m, n_samples = 1000, warmup = 1000, chains = 4)  # Convenience wrappers draws <- nuts(m, n_samples = 1000) draws <- hmc(m, n_samples = 1000)  # MAP optimisation — find the posterior mode fit <- opt(m) fit$par  # Laplace approximation — Gaussian centred at MAP la <- laplace(m) la$mean la$sd  # Variational inference (ADVI) — mean-field or full-rank Gaussian vi_fit <- variational(m, method = "meanfield") vi_fit$mean vi_fit$draws  # samples from the variational posterior

title: Migrating from greta to gretaR
text: gretaR includes gretaR_glm(), high-level convenience function fitting Bayesian generalised linear models using standard R formula syntax: equivalent greta — models must built DSL.
code: # Bayesian linear regression in one call fit <- gretaR_glm(   Sepal.Length ~ Sepal.Width + Petal.Length,   data = iris,   family = "gaussian" ) summary(fit$draws)  # Logistic regression fit <- gretaR_glm(y ~ x1 + x2, data = dat, family = "binomial")  # With custom priors fit <- gretaR_glm(   y ~ x,   data = dat,   family = "gaussian",   prior = list(beta = normal(0, 1), sigma = half_cauchy(1)) )

what: Fit a Bayesian GLM Using Formula Syntax — gretaR_glm
title: Fit a Bayesian GLM Using Formula Syntax — gretaR_glm
text: high-level interface specifying fitting generalised linear models (including mixed / hierarchical models) using standard R formula syntax. Internally translates gretaR DSL, constructs model, runs MCMC inference. lme4-style random effects supported via regex parsing (lme4 required). Recognised patterns: (1|group) — random intercepts group (x|group) — correlated random intercepts + slopes (0 + x|group) — random slopes (intercept) Multiple random effect terms permitted, e.g. y ~ x + (1|site) + (1|year). non-centred parameterisation used default superior HMC geometry.

title: Fit a Bayesian GLM Using Formula Syntax — gretaR_glm
code: gretaR_glm(   formula,   data,   family = c("gaussian", "binomial", "poisson"),   prior = NULL,   sampler = c("nuts", "hmc", "vi", "map"),   chains = 4L,   iter = 2000L,   warmup = NULL,   formula_style = NULL,   verbose = TRUE,   ... )

title: Fit a Bayesian GLM Using Formula Syntax — gretaR_glm
text: formula formula specifying model (e.g., y ~ x1 + x2, y ~ x + (1|group) mixed models). data data frame containing variables referenced formula. family Distribution family: "gaussian" (default), "binomial", "poisson". prior named list gretaR distribution objects parameter priors. Recognised names: "beta" (regression coefficients), "intercept", "sigma" (residual SD, gaussian ), "tau" (random effect SD). Use NULL default priors. sampler Sampler: "nuts" (default), "hmc", "vi", "map". chains Number MCMC chains (default 4). iter Total iterations per chain (warmup + samples, default 2000). warmup Number warmup iterations (default half iter). formula_style Optional explicit formula style hint: "base", "lme4", "brms", "mgcv". NULL (default), auto-detected. verbose Logical; print progress (default TRUE). ... Additional arguments passed sampler.

title: Fit a Bayesian GLM Using Formula Syntax — gretaR_glm

title: gretaR 0.1.0
text: Continuous: normal(), half_normal(), half_cauchy(), student_t(), cauchy(), exponential(), gamma_dist(), beta_dist(), lognormal(), uniform(), multivariate_normal(). Discrete: bernoulli(), binomial_dist(), poisson_dist(), negative_binomial(). Multivariate: dirichlet(), lkj_correlation(), wishart(). Custom: custom_distribution() — user-defined torch log-probability. Mixture: mixture() — log-sum-exp marginalisation discrete components. Truncation: truncation = c(lower, upper) continuous distributions (greta-compatible syntax).

title: gretaR 0.1.0
text: NUTS static HMC windowed warmup adaptation. Variational inference (variational()) — mean-field full-rank ADVI. MAP estimation (opt()) via Adam optimiser. Laplace approximation (laplace()) Hessian-based posterior covariance. Stan backend: mcmc(m, backend = "stan") 30–150x faster inference standard models via cmdstanr.

title: gretaR 0.1.0
text: gretaR_glm() — high-level GLM specification gaussian, binomial, poisson families. lme4-style random effects: (1|group), (x|group), (0+x|group). mgcv-style smooth terms: s(), te(), ti(), t2() via smooth2random decomposition (21 mgcv basis types supported). Auto-detection formula style (base, lme4, mgcv).

title: gretaR 0.1.0
text: Array indexing alpha[group_id] group-level parameters. Non-centred parameterisation default formula interface.

title: gretaR 0.1.0
text: Sparse matrix support via Matrix package (as_data() accepts dgCMatrix). Compiled log-probability function 3–4x torch backend speedup. Unified gretaR_fit output print(), summary(), coef(), plot(). seed parameter inference functions reproducibility. compile_to_stan() inspecting generated Stan code.

title: gretaR 0.1.0
text: 5 vignettes: complete guide, getting started, GLMs, hierarchical models, migration greta. JOSS paper draft. Technical documentation (.pdf, .tex, .md).
